_note: Curated list of key federal bills for the 119th Congress (2025-2027). Each entry maps a bill to its final-passage vote IDs in federal-member-votes.json. Use summaryOverride in member.html if needed. Add Senate voteId when available.

bills.title: Laken Riley Act
bills.summary: Requires U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to detain undocumented immigrants who are charged with or convicted of theft, burglary, or violent crimes. Named for Laken Riley, a Georgia nursing student killed in February 2024 by a Venezuelan national who had entered the U.S. illegally. The first bill signed into law by President Trump in his second term.

bills.title: Full-Year Continuing Appropriations and Extensions Act, 2025
bills.summary: Funded the federal government through September 30, 2025 at largely flat spending levels, avoiding a government shutdown. Extended dozens of expiring programs. Passed the House 217–213 and the Senate 54–46, largely along party lines. Democrats objected to the absence of a debt ceiling fix and cuts to some domestic programs.

bills.title: HALT Fentanyl Act
bills.summary: Permanently classifies fentanyl analogues as Schedule I controlled substances under federal law, making them subject to the most stringent drug penalties regardless of minor chemical variations. Supporters argue it closes loopholes exploited by drug traffickers. Critics raised concerns about its impact on research and on communities facing addiction.

bills.title: TAKE IT DOWN Act
bills.summary: Makes it a federal crime to publish non-consensual intimate images, including AI-generated deepfakes. Requires online platforms to remove such content within 48 hours of a victim's request. Passed the House 409–2 and the Senate unanimously — one of the rare bipartisan landslides of the 119th Congress. Signed into law by President Trump.

bills.title: "One Big Beautiful Bill" — Tax Cuts & Spending Reconciliation
bills.summary: Landmark reconciliation package extending the 2017 Trump tax cuts, adding new deductions (no tax on tips, overtime, and seniors' Social Security income), funding border enforcement and deportations, and raising the debt ceiling by $5 trillion. Reduces Medicaid spending and eliminates most clean energy tax credits from the Inflation Reduction Act. Passed the House 218–214 and Senate 51–50 (VP Vance broke the tie), entirely along party lines.

bills.title: GENIUS Act — Stablecoin Regulation
bills.summary: Establishes the first federal regulatory framework for payment stablecoins (cryptocurrencies pegged to a stable asset like the U.S. dollar). Requires issuers to hold 1:1 reserves in safe assets and comply with anti-money-laundering rules. Passed the Senate 68–30 and the House 308–122, with bipartisan support. Supporters call it essential for U.S. digital finance leadership; critics warned it legitimizes crypto for payments prematurely.

bills.title: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026
bills.summary: Authorizes $895 billion in defense spending for FY2026 — the annual bill that sets Pentagon policy and funding priorities. Includes pay raises for troops, funds for shipbuilding, AI and cybersecurity programs, and provisions related to military base closures. Passed the House 312–112 and the Senate with broad bipartisan support.

bills.title: Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2026
bills.summary: Funded the entire federal government through September 30, 2026 — a full-year omnibus spending package after months of stopgap continuing resolutions. Passed the House 217–214 and the Senate 52–47. Funded domestic agencies near FY2025 levels while increasing defense spending; Democrats objected to cuts in some social programs.
